skill.name: react-native-dev
skill.description: Practical React Native and Expo development guide for building mobile apps, covering UI, navigation, state, forms, networking, performance, testing, native capabilities, and release workflows.
skill.summary: Build production-ready React Native and Expo apps with focused implementation patterns.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: The static score is inflated by Markdown code spans, route examples, environment-variable examples, and documentation snippets that do not execute by themselves. The skill is a legitimate React Native and Expo guide with no prompt-injection text found, but it includes shell commands and a pipe-to-shell installer example that warrant a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-shell installer example
medium_findings.description: The testing reference recommends installing Maestro with a curl command piped directly to bash. This is a real risky pattern because it executes remote content without review, although it appears to be a documentation example rather than malicious skill behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicitly present and is a known risky installation pattern. Confidence is below very high because the command targets the vendor installer in documentation and there is no evidence of hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Executable project setup commands in guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes npx, cd, Expo install, EAS, and test commands that an assistant may suggest for a user project. These commands are expected for a development guide, but users should review them before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real and could modify a project or environment. The surrounding context is normal React Native and Expo setup guidance, so the risk is operational rather than clearly malicious.

low_findings.title: Markdown code spans flagged as Ruby backticks
low_findings.description: Most external-command findings are false positives caused by inline Markdown code and fenced examples. The cited lines describe React Native APIs and reference links, not Ruby shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is visibly Markdown documentation and TypeScript JSX examples. There is no Ruby file, interpreter invocation, or runtime backtick execution path in these locations.

low_findings.title: Environment-variable examples are instructional
low_findings.description: Environment file and secret findings come from examples explaining Expo public variables, server-only secrets, and safe handling. The references warn not to expose database credentials or write-capable API keys.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment-variable references are documentation examples, and one section explicitly warns against exposing sensitive credentials. Placeholder secret syntax still deserves low-risk disclosure for marketplace users.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and keyword alerts are benign examples
low_findings.description: Path traversal, memory-mapped file, weak-crypto, and keylogger alerts are caused by relative import examples, performance terminology, and UI labels. No file access or surveillance behavior is implemented by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected locations are explanatory text or UI enum labels. They do not contain code that reads arbitrary files, captures keystrokes, or performs cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote script execution through shell pipe
dangerous_patterns.description: The command downloads an installer over HTTPS and pipes it to bash. This should not be executed automatically by an assistant without user confirmation and source review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact shell pipe is present in a bash block and is a well-known risky execution pattern. It is a vendor install instruction, not evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dependency and deployment commands in examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes package installation, app scaffolding, build, deploy, and environment-setting examples. These are normal for Expo guidance but can change a user workspace or remote project configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and executable, but the documentation context is legitimate. The pattern is dangerous only if executed without user review.

content.value_statement: React Native projects require many connected decisions across navigation, state, testing, performance, and release tooling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Expo patterns for building and shipping mobile apps.
actual_capabilities: Guides new Expo project setup with Expo Router and TypeScript conventions.
Provides patterns for components, styling, animations, navigation, forms, and state management.
Explains networking, authentication tokens, API routes, environment variables, and offline behavior.
Covers performance work for lists, images, startup time, bundle size, rendering, and animations.
Includes testing guidance for Jest, React Native Testing Library, mocked modules, and Maestro E2E flows.
Documents native capabilities such as camera, location, haptics, notifications, biometrics, and permissions.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not run React Native builds by itself.
Examples may need updates for project-specific Expo SDK versions and package choices.
Native module guidance can require custom development builds outside Expo Go.
Security-sensitive code such as authentication, webhooks, and secrets still needs project review.

use_cases.title: Start an Expo App
use_cases.description: Create a new TypeScript Expo project with routing, common dependencies, and base folder conventions.

use_cases.title: Improve App Quality
use_cases.description: Add testing, form validation, data fetching, and performance fixes to an existing React Native codebase.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Release
use_cases.description: Plan SDK upgrades, EAS builds, app store submission, CI checks, and production environment setup.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the React Native dev skill to plan a new Expo TypeScript app with Expo Router, basic tabs, styling, and testing.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Feature Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the React Native dev skill to design a feature screen with navigation, form validation, server state, loading states, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the React Native dev skill to review this app for list jank, image loading, startup time, bundle size, and animation performance.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Production Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the React Native dev skill to create a release checklist for Expo SDK upgrade, EAS build, CI testing, secrets, and store submission.

output_examples.input: Plan a new Expo app for a marketplace product.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step setup plan with routing, folder structure, shared UI, data fetching, environment variables, and first test coverage.

output_examples.input: Review a slow product list screen.
output: A prioritized diagnosis of rendering, list virtualization, image loading, and memoization issues.
Specific changes to improve scroll smoothness and reduce unnecessary re-renders.

output_examples.input: Prepare an app for production release.
output_examples.output: A release checklist covering SDK compatibility, EAS profiles, environment handling, automated tests, store assets, and final verification.

best_practices: Review shell commands before running them, especially commands that install packages or deploy services.
Match each feature request to the relevant reference file before generating implementation details.
Test critical navigation, authentication, forms, and checkout paths before app store submission.
anti_patterns: Do not treat every example as a drop-in solution without checking the app SDK version and dependencies.
Do not expose database credentials or write-capable API keys through EXPO_PUBLIC variables.
Do not run remote installer scripts or deployment commands without user approval and source review.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps build, test, optimize, and ship React Native and Expo apps using practical implementation guidance.

faq.question: Does it support both Claude and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it create a complete mobile app automatically?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and implementation guidance, but the final app still depends on project requirements and human review.

faq.question: Does it cover Expo Router?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes routing guidance for stacks, tabs, native tabs, modals, links, sheets, and route conventions.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is mainly documentation, but it includes shell commands and a pipe-to-shell installer example.

faq.question: How should teams use this skill safely?
faq.answer: Use it for guidance, then review commands, dependencies, environment variables, and release steps before execution.
